In terms of Forward P/E, Stellantis is actually valued more richly than Ford Motor Company (F) and General Motors Company (GM). Its net margin, which has suffered for the past quarters, still stands negative on a TTM basis (however, the company is guiding a low single digits operating margin for H2 2025, as shown below).

Where Stellantis shines is once again their positive net debt position. This is unique for an automaker (no major global carmakers enjoy a positive net debt position), due to the CapEx heavy nature of the industry. It is definitely a strong positive for Stellantis. The limited cost of servicing its debt will compound the diminished CapEx need to launch new American trucks and muscle cars. I think this makes it highly likely we will see positive FCF in 2026, together with an increase in margins.

All eyes on Free Cash Flow

Until early 2024, Stellantis enjoyed double-digits, industry-leading margins. I think we are unlikely to see the company returning to those days, as the world is very different now. Those margins were on the back of a strong post-pandemic demand for vehicles, compounded by a chip shortage that gave strong pricing power to carmakers.

Unfortunately the company ended up not capitalizing on that situation, rather focusing on improving short term financial metrics at the expense of losing its competitiveness in North America. The situation was only exacerbated by tariffs imposed by the Trump administration, which are mentioned by Stellantis as a headwind in their latest earnings report.

Today, the real focus for a turnaround should be, in my view, on Cash Flow. The company’s Levered Free Cash Flow has been on a steady decline for the past years, as the chart below shows (quarterly data from Seeking Alpha).

It is pretty obvious that, long term, a company cannot function (let alone represent a compelling investment proposition) with negative cash flow and breaking-even on margins. Luckily for Stellantis, the company had a strong cash position it mostly inherited from the Fiat Chrysler side of the merger. Stellantis used its cash to navigate the last few quarters without having to take on major debt or diluting shareholders (shares count is stable since the birth of STLA).

However, time is running out. While the company still has ~$22 Billion in cash and enjoys a net debt position, as an investor I want to see their new product strategy working out (especially in North America) and FCF and margin improving. Only then will I be able to recommend a Buy.

Risks: about (and beyond) North America

For the year 2024, Stellantis generated 59% of their AOI (Adjusted Operating Income) in North America, 35% in Enlarged Europe and the remaining 6% in the rest of the world (with a negative AOI from Maserati of about half a billion).

It is clear from these figures that Stellantis is very dependent on North America. At the same time, the company has most of its workforce in Europe, where many of its plants are serving a struggling and shrinking European car market, and political forces make it difficult for the company to become leaner (Stellantis is partially owned by both French and Italian governmental entities).

This, in my view, is the main risk of investing in Stellantis today. The company has a tricky geopolitical exposure, compounded by the current US administration and its unconventional trade policy.

The fact the company has minimal exposure to any region outside of North America and Europe is in itself another risk. While that served Stellantis well in the past (its nonexistent presence in China means the company did not suffer from losing market share there like its European peers), this fact could turn from tailwind to headwind in a rapidly changing geopolitical landscape.

Further elements worth considering before investing (or not) in Stellantis concern autonomous driving. Robotaxis are mentioned by Stellantis in their latest earnings report (see below).

Robotaxis could represent an opportunity or a threat for Stellantis. For example, the company could capitalize on its U.S. presence (where regulation and experimentation on robotaxis is advanced) to eventually bring the same technology in Europe as a leader. Conversely, robotaxis could represent a broad industry headwind as they could theoretically drastically reduce demand for consumer vehicles. Readers can decide for themselves what is more likely.

Personally, I see the automaking industry as a very tough business and one where, as an investor, I hardly want to have much exposure to. For Stellantis specifically, I find any bull case based on robotaxis to be stretched, given we are talking about a company with negative FCF and barely breaking even. Stellantis needs to fix the basics before it can dream about the role it wants to play in the future of mobility.

Conclusion

The market has correctly re-priced Stellantis based on industry tailwinds concerning an ease on U.S. emission standards. This is great for investors who had STLA in their portfolio, but in my view it does not represent an interesting entry point. These tailwinds concern the whole auto industry (at least those car makers exposed to the USA). But Stellantis still has a lot to show.

The company says it will launch five new vehicles in America in the next few months. But how those vehicles will sell, what their financials could look like and whether they will be able to compete in the market and bring the company back to profitability, remains to be seen.

In many of my previous articles on Stellantis, I focused on discussing the product portfolio and the strategic plans of this company, especially for what concerns North America. But I cannot do that today, as we are still too early in the turnaround plan of CEO Antonio Filosa. That's why I reiterate my Hold rating for the company.

I will keep covering Stellantis and assess whether a Buy is warranted once the relaunch plan evolves and new vehicles hit the market.

data.title: The Future Of Forum Energy Technologies Looks Much Brighter Than Its Past
data.description:
Forum Energy Technologies (FET) is one of the top-performing stocks in the entire stock market this year. To be sure, it has surged 106% this year and thus it has outperformed the S&P 500 (+16%) by an impressive margin. On the one hand, as the stock has gone up in an almost straight line, its short-term momentum appears positive. On the other hand, after such a spectacular performance, it is only natural that some investors may fear that the stock has become overvalued and risky. In this article, I will analyze why Forum Energy Technologies remains attractive, despite its steep rally.

Business overview

Forum Energy Technologies is a global company that serves the oil, natural gas and renewable energy industries. It designs, manufactures and sells products that increase the safety and efficiency of energy exploration and production. The company was founded in 2005 and is headquartered in Texas.

Forum Energy Technologies has a terrible performance history. To be sure, it has incurred material losses in 8 of the last 9 years. As a result, the stock dramatically underperformed the broad market until last year. To provide a perspective, the stock plunged 96% from the end of 2015 to early 2020, when the coronavirus crisis struck. Even after its extraordinary rally this year, the stock is still trading 87% lower than its level a decade ago. A markedly poor history always raises a red flag and thus investors should perform their due diligence with special attention before buying this stock.

Fortunately, the future looks much brighter than the past for Forum Energy Technologies. In the third quarter, the company saw its revenue decrease 2% sequentially but grew its adjusted EBITDA 13% sequentially, primarily thanks to cost savings. It thus posted adjusted earnings per share of $0.27. It also enjoyed strong demand in the offshore and international markets, and thus its backlog surged 21%, to a 10-year high level. This certainly bodes well for future growth prospects.

Indeed, management stated that it expects the strong backlog, cost reductions and market share gains to provide a tailwind to the performance of the company in 2026. It also provided remarkably strong guidance for this year, expecting free cash flow of $70-$80 million. At the mid-point, this guidance corresponds to free cash flow per share of $6.15. In other words, the stock is currently trading at just 5.2 times its expected free cash flow this year.

Moreover, Forum Energy Technologies has a highly promising 5-year growth plan in place.

Source: Investor Presentation

Thanks to global economic growth, urbanization and fast-growing energy demand from data centers, the company expects the demand in its markets to grow 9% per year on average over the next five years. It thus expects its annual revenues to double to $1.6 billion by 2030.

In addition, management expects free cash flow to approximately triple until 2030, from $75 million this year to $200-$230 million.

Source: Investor Presentation

At the current share count of 12.2 million, the expected free cash flow of Forum Energy Technologies in 2030 corresponds to free cash flow per share of about $17.60. This is more than half of the current stock price of $31.90. It is thus evident that Forum Energy Technologies has great upside potential if it meets its own guidance.

Analysts seem to agree on the promising growth prospects of the stock. They expect the company to switch from a loss per share of -$0.71 this year to earnings per share of $1.23 in 2026 and grow its earnings per share to $3.25 in 2027.

Debt

The oil and gas industry is infamous for its dramatic cyclicality. It is thus crucial for a company to have a strong balance sheet in order to endure the downturns of this industry. Fortunately, Forum Energy Technologies has learned its lesson from its excessive losses in the past, which were partly caused by a high debt load.

The company has reduced its leverage ratio (Net Debt to EBITDA) to 1.3x, which is certainly healthy. In addition, its net debt (as per Buffett’s formula: Net debt = total liabilities – cash – receivables) is standing at $278 million. This amount is 75% of the market capitalization of the stock and hence it is reasonable.

Notably, net interest expense has exceeded operating income in the last 12 months ($20.5 million vs. $14.8 million). This indicates that the company is not in a rock-solid financial position. Nevertheless, if it keeps growing its free cash flow as per its guidance, it will almost certainly be able to service its debt without any liquidity issues.

Valuation

As mentioned above, analysts expect Forum Energy Technologies to achieve earnings per share of $1.23 in 2026 and $3.25 in 2027. This means that the stock is currently trading at 25.9 times its expected earnings in 2026 and 9.8 times its expected earnings in 2027. The latter is much lower than the median forward price-to-earnings ratio of 13.5x of the energy sector.

On the one hand, as we are two years apart from the end of 2027 and given the poor performance record of the company, a somewhat cautious valuation level is warranted. On the other hand, it is important to realize that the company expects to keep growing beyond 2027, until at least 2030, as per its 5-year plan. As a result, it is likely to grow its earnings per share even further in the years 2028-2030, in the absence of a downturn of the energy industry.

It is also remarkable that the stock is exceptionally cheaply valued relative to its expected free cash flow. The stock is currently trading at only 5.2 times its expected free cash flow this year and only 1.8 times its expected free cash flow in 2030. To cut a long story short, Forum Energy Technologies deserves to trade at a discount to its sector due to its poor performance record, but it appears deeply undervalued given its exciting growth potential.

Risks

The first risk factor has already been mentioned; it is the poor performance record of Forum Energy Technologies. Some investors choose to ignore the past performance of a company, as it belongs to history, but a poor record always raises a red flag regarding the risk of poor business execution in the future. On the bright side, Forum Energy Technologies seems to have learned its lesson from its history and appears to have a promising 5-year growth plan in place.

The other risk factor is the deteriorating outlook of the oil market. OPEC stuck to strict production quotas in recent years in order to support the price of oil but it is now in the process of restoring its output aggressively, with a goal to boost it by 2.0 million barrels per day until the end of next year. As a result, the price of oil has declined towards a 4-year low lately.

Due to the ramp-up of production of the members of OPEC, the Energy Information Administration [EIA] has provided a bearish outlook, expecting the average price of oil to decrease 20% next year, from $69 in 2025 to $55. If this occurs, drilling activity is likely to decrease significantly, hence the business of Forum Energy Technologies will probably be affected.

While the base near-term scenario is negative for the global oil industry, investors should keep in mind that Forum Energy Technologies has a promising 5-year plan in place. The oil industry is so cyclical that a downturn within the next 5 years is almost inevitable. However, investors should be prepared to endure such a downturn and remain focused on the 5-year plan of the company.

Of course, this is much easier said than done. The stock price of Forum Energy Technologies is likely to come under pressure whenever the next downturn of the energy sector shows up. Therefore, the stock is suitable only for investors who can endure stock price pressure and remain focused on the long-term prospects of the stock.

Final thoughts

One of the primary investing criteria of legendary investor Warren Buffett is a solid performance record. Indeed, one can draw some conclusions about the future of a company by examining its past performance. The past performance of Forum Energy Technologies has been daunting, as the stock vastly underperformed the broad market in the decade leading to last year. However, the company seems to have turned a page and has provided a bright outlook for the next five years. Based on the potential of the growth plan, the stock appears deeply undervalued, and thus it is likely to highly reward shareholders in the upcoming years.

data.title: 3 REITs That Big Money Might Snap Up Soon
data.description:
Real estate investment trust, or REIT (VNQ), sector valuations have dropped so low that they have recently become the target of various activist investment firms and private equity companies.

They build stakes in the REITs and then push them to sell assets, buy back shares, and, in select cases, even go private via buyouts to unlock value.

Here are some examples of that in recent months:

  • Plymouth Industrial REIT (PLYM) agreed to be acquired at a 50% premium by private equity company Makarora.
  • Sotherly Hotels (SOHO) is going to be acquired at a huge 150% premium by Kemmons Wilson Hospitality Partners and Ascendant Capital Partners.
  • City Office (CIO) announced a merger with MCME Carell at a 20%+ premium.
  • Whitestone REIT (WSR) received yet another offer to be acquired at a 21% premium.
  • National Storage REIT (NSR/OTC:NTSGF) just received an offer from Brookfield (BAM), led by the legendary investor Bruce Flatt, to be acquired at a 26% premium.
  • Centerspace (CSR) surged in value after it announced that it had hired an advisor to explore a sale of the entire company.

My prediction is that we will see many more REITs get bought out or announce strategic alternatives to unlock value over the coming quarters.

They have been in a bear market for nearly 4 years now, with valuations persistently trading at steep discounts to private market values, and management teams are finally losing patience and becoming more receptive to exploring strategic alternatives.

At the same time, activists and private equity firms see an opportunity to squeeze profits from REITs, even as the rest of the market is fairly expensive.

Here are three REITs we own at High Yield Landlord that could profit from this:

Big Yellow Group (BYG/OTCPK:BYLOF)

Big Yellow Group is the leading self-storage REIT in the UK.

I have personally owned it for about 10 years, first buying it following the Brexit crash, and I hope to hold it for another decade to come.

Self-storage has historically been the most rewarding REIT sector in the U.S., earning nearly 20% average annual total returns over the past decades.

They were so rewarding because the concept was growing in popularity, and these REITs could develop new properties, earning large spreads over their cost of capital.

Big Yellow is now replicating this same model in the UK, which is still 20 years behind. The concept is just now growing in popularity, but there is still 10x less storage space per capita in the UK than in the U.S.:

This has already resulted in very attractive returns for Big Yellow shareholders over the past decades, and given that we are still early, we expect this strong performance to continue for a long time to come:

But rumors recently began circulating that Blackstone (BX) might be interested in buying out Big Yellow Group (BYG/BYLOF).

This led the stock to surge by about 20% in just a few days:

I think that the REIT has more to give.

This initial 20% surge was only based on rumors. No official offer has even been made, and yet, investors are ready to speculate enough to send the stock 20% higher.

This suggests to me that if and when they get an official offer, the premium will be large enough to justify even more upside, especially given that its stock remains historically cheap following the recent surge.

At its peak in early 2022, it traded at nearly 40x AFFO, and that's down to just 19x today:

40x was likely excessive.

But 19x is arguably too low when you consider that the REIT has very little leverage with a low 10% LTV, and it owns a lot of undeveloped land that's not yet generating any cash flow.

Big Yellow has historically traded at these high multiples due to these reasons. Low leverage coupled with rapid growth justifies higher valuations.

I think that long-term fair value is at right around 25x, and Blackstone would likely be happy to acquire it in the low-to-mid 20s just to gain control of the self-storage platform to then scale it across Europe.

Big Yellow Group has by far the most recognizable brand in the UK, and this has a lot of value to big players like Blackstone as they look to expand into this new property sector:

So in short, we think that a buyout attempt is likely here and could result in more upside.

But if not, we are happy to keep holding onto Big Yellow for the long run. It is still slightly discounted and offers a path to double-digit annual total returns when you combine its 4% dividend yield and 8% long-term expected average annual growth rate.

Rexford Industrial Realty, Inc. (REXR)

Rexford Industrial is one of the leading industrial REITs in the U.S.:

It surged recently after news came out that the famous activist investor Elliot Management had taken a substantial stake in the company.

Their investment was so big that they have now become one of the biggest investors in the company:

Elliott is one of the most renowned activists in the world, commonly targeting undervalued companies and then pushing them to unlock value via major M&A deals.

As an example, Elliott was recently involved in the take-private deal of City Office REIT, which is one of the latest REIT buyouts.

Is Rexford now their next target?

On paper, there are a lot of factors that would make Rexford a great target for a buyout.

It trades at a steep discount to private market values, and that's despite owning very desirable assets, having a strong balance sheet, and having a shareholder-friendly management team.

Even then, I don't think that a buyout is the first step here due to one simple reason: Rexford is very big, owning about $20 billion worth of properties.

That's likely too big a deal even for the biggest alternative asset managers like Blackstone.

Therefore, a buyout likely isn't their first intention.

I think that it is likelier that they will try to push Rexford to sell some of their assets at private market values and then reinvest the proceeds in large buybacks to create and unlock value. If that's not enough, they can then push for a potential buyout once the company has become smaller in size.

This is exactly what they have been doing already. So far this year, they have sold $166 million worth of assets and bought back $100 million worth of shares, and they proudly display this on their latest investor deck.

I think that Elliott is seeing this and thinking that they can pressure them to simply double down on this capital allocation plan and push them to sell assets more aggressively to buy back stock.

Private market cap rates for some of its top assets are in the mid-4s. They are so low because these are truly irreplaceable assets in strategic locations that are already fully built out. Moreover, their lease rates are often deeply below market, offering an opportunity for sizable rent bumps in the future.

In comparison, its own stock is trading at a high-5 implied cap rate.

Therefore, selling assets in the private market to buy back stock in the public market would be highly accretive and immediately expand its FFO and NAV per share.

This is what Farmland Partners (FPI) and BSR REIT (HOM.U:CA) have been doing in recent years to create value.

But the difference between Rexford and these smaller REITs is that it has a far stronger balance sheet, which could allow it to be even more aggressive.

Its current loan-to-value is just about 25%, a historic low. It also has limited debt maturities over the coming year.

This means that the REIT could also further accelerate buybacks by taking on some leverage. With its BBB+ rated balance sheet, it could likely secure long-term debt with an interest rate in the high 4s to buy back shares in the high 5s, resulting in immediate accretion. It could then take its time in gradually selling assets to pay off this leverage.

I would bet that this is the plan.

I don't think that Elliott will seek to sell the whole company, at least for now. It appears more likely to me that they will simply push the management to double down on buybacks as a first step.

Their $100 million buybacks year-to-date are a positive sign, but they don't truly move the needle for a company of this scale.

However, if they did 5-10x more buybacks, it would create meaningful value and also likely help the market sentiment of the stock.

This is likely why Elliott got interested.

We also own a position at High Yield Landlord, as our fair value estimate is about 30% higher than the current share price. While we wait for the upside, we earn a 4.2% dividend yield, and we expect the company to grow its cash flow by 6-8% annually over the long run.

Whitestone REIT

I just recently highlighted Whitestone REIT in a separate article that you can read by clicking here, so I will keep this one short.

The REIT has become one of the clearest buyout targets in the REIT sector, attracting several offers over the past year.

The reason why is simple: Retail properties are today undersupplied and enjoy steady growth. WSR happens to own some of the most desirable retail properties in its peer group, and yet, it trades at the lowest valuation relative to its net asset value:

Its stock is trading at a near 9% implied cap rate, but we think that its assets would be worth closer to a 6-6.5% cap rate in the private market, resulting in a steep discount.

So far, the buyout offers have been rejected because they would undervalue the company, and I agree with the management.

The last offer came in at $15.20, which is far below our $17-20 NAV per share estimate. The discount is especially steep when you consider that the company's NAV is likely to grow significantly in the coming years as its same-property NOI keeps on growing and interest rates return to lower levels, potentially resulting in some cap rate compression.

Therefore, it would not surprise me if Whitestone soon received yet another offer at a higher price. I just hope that the management will remain firm and not sell out for a small short-term profit, because the long-term prospects seem very compelling here.

We have gotten to interview their management several times at High Yield Landlord and hold them in high regard.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
